metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the test suite (e.g., `pytest tests/behavioral/ -v --tb=short` targeting a named file/directory) and ensure each AC bullet names the test function or fixture that proves it.
findings.finding: No proving commands are specified for any of the five ACs — there is no test file path, no pytest invocation, and no CI integration point described. An agent completing this work has no way to produce concrete evidence that each AC passes, satisfying E6(a).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section — all five bullets
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Proving command' subsection: `pytest tests/behavioral/test_ws5_invariants.py -v` and annotate each AC with the corresponding test function name (e.g., `test_idempotency`, `test_hot_path_regression`, `test_concurrency_serialization`, `test_no_mutate`, `test_absent_marker`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a forward-reference to the WS1 `run_ensures` interface (module path, signature, and the subprocess-suppression invariant it must uphold) in AC2, so the hot-path regression test has a grounded anchor to instrument rather than asserting against a not-yet-existing symbol.
findings.finding: AC2's hot-path regression test makes three specific behavioral assertions about `run_ensures` and `.ensure-applied` (never called, no git-config subprocess spawned, read at most once per process), but neither `run_ensures` nor `.ensure-applied` exist anywhere in `src/rebar/` — they are WS1 deliverables. The plan gives no forward-reference to the WS1 interface contract (module path, subprocess-suppression invariant), so a test author has no grounded anchor to instrument or mock, and the assertions risk being vacuously satisfied by the mere absence of the symbol rather than by an actual guard in the hot path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: Hot-path regression test

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC2: Resolve the unverified capability assertion — specify the subprocess-interception mechanism (e.g., monkeypatch `subprocess.run` and `subprocess.Popen`) and confirm that WS1's `run_ensures` is constrained to use only those entry-points, making the AC testable by construction.
findings.finding: The hot-path AC asserts 'spawns no git-config subprocess' as a behavioral invariant the test must prove, but the plan never names the mechanism by which the test intercepts or counts subprocess spawns. This is an unverified capability assertion about test feasibility: the standard interception approach (monkeypatching `subprocess.run`/`subprocess.Popen`) is valid only if `run_ensures` uses the stdlib subprocess module rather than, e.g., `os.execvp` or a C extension — a property of the WS1 deliverable not yet specified.
Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC3: Explicitly mandate that the concurrency test uses two real OS-level subprocesses (following the `test_store_concurrency.py` pattern with an on-PATH editable `rebar`) and document WHY (fcntl.flock is per-process, not per-thread/fork).
findings.finding: The concurrency AC names `_store/lock.py` as the serialization primitive but does not mandate real OS subprocesses for the test. The codebase's established prior art (`tests/integration/test_store_concurrency.py`) always uses `subprocess.run` against an editable on-PATH `rebar` precisely because `fcntl.flock` (the actual lock mechanism) is per-process — it does NOT serialize threads within the same process, nor does `multiprocessing.Process` (fork) inherit the lock correctly on all platforms. A thread- or fork-based test would yield a structurally false-green that never exercises the actual serialization invariant.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'The test MUST spawn two subprocesses via `subprocess.run` (not threads or `multiprocessing.Process`) against an editable on-PATH `rebar`, following the pattern in `tests/integration/test_store_concurrency.py`, because `fcntl.flock` serializes OS processes only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the self-contradictory naming parenthetical in the Why section to state the intended term unambiguously (e.g., 'Naming: this work uses the term X, not Y — consistent with WS2').
findings.finding: The Why section contains a self-contradictory naming clarification: '(Naming: this is the "pending-hint", consistent with WS2 — not "pending-hint".)' — it says the thing IS pending-hint and NOT pending-hint in the same sentence, which is confusing and could mislead an executor about terminology.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with a clear statement such as: '(Naming: the feature is called "pending-hint" throughout, consistent with WS2.)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a rate-limit AC (with a concrete verifiable behavior, e.g. 'sweep respects a configurable rate ceiling and does not retry-storm') or remove 'rate-limit' from the Why sharp-edges list if it is intentionally out of scope for WS5.
findings.finding: 'rate-limit' is listed in the Why section as a sharp edge the tests should lock down, but there is no corresponding test in the What description or ACs, leaving it an orphan concern with zero coverage.
findings.location: Why section ('rate-limit') vs What section and all 5 ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Add to What and ACs: 'Rate-limit regression test: the sweep respects the configured per-run rate ceiling; a synthetic run that would exceed the limit stops at the ceiling and does not retry-storm.' Or, if rate-limiting is enforced elsewhere, add a single line to Why: 'rate-limit enforcement is tested in WS[N]'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the naming parenthetical in Why to read, e.g., '(Naming: this is the "pending-hint", consistent with WS2 — not "<rejected-alias>")' with the actual rejected alias spelled out.
findings.finding: The naming parenthetical in Why reads '(Naming: this is the "pending-hint", consistent with WS2 — not "pending-hint".)' — it asserts the name IS 'pending-hint' and simultaneously NOT 'pending-hint', which is self-contradictory and unreadable.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical with the intended contrast, e.g. '(Naming: this is the "pending-hint" feature, consistent with WS2 — not "deferred-hint" or "lazy-hint".)' using whichever alias was meant to be rejected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the garbled naming note in the 'Why' section to clearly state the canonical feature name (e.g., 'pending-hint') and remove the self-contradicting negation so cross-WS naming is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The 'Why' section contains a self-contradictory, garbled naming note — 'this is the "pending-hint", consistent with WS2 — not "pending-hint"' — which says the same term twice and contradicts itself, leaving the canonical name for the feature undefined and likely stranding any reader who relies on this for cross-WS traceability.
findings.location: ## Why — last sentence: '(Naming: this is the "pending-hint", consistent with WS2 — not "pending-hint".'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical with: '(Naming: the feature is called "pending-hint" throughout WS2-5; ensure test module and fixture names use this exact term.)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the garbled naming note in the Why section: state the correct canonical term (e.g., 'pending-hint') and what it replaces or aligns with in WS2, so implementers choose consistent names.
findings.finding: The Why section contains a self-contradictory naming note — 'this is the "pending-hint", consistent with WS2 — not "pending-hint"' — which says the plan IS and IS NOT 'pending-hint' in the same clause. This garbled text is confusing to implementers who need to know the correct terminology when naming test files, fixtures, and assertions.
findings.location: Why section, sentence beginning '(Naming: this is the "pending-hint"...'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical with something like: '(Naming: this plan uses the term "pending-hint" consistently with WS2; do not use any other alias.)' — or if the intent was to correct a prior name, write: 'this is called X (not Y) — consistent with WS2.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Amend AC3 to explicitly require real OS-level subprocesses (e.g., `subprocess.Popen` or `multiprocessing.Process`) for the concurrency test — not threads — so that fcntl.flock inter-process serialization is actually exercised, matching the existing subprocess-spawn pattern used in the codebase.
findings.finding: AC3's concurrency test can be implemented using threads (within the same PID), which would always pass trivially because fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB) — the actual lock mechanism in lock.py — is per-process granular: threads within one PID share the flock and never contend. The AC says 'two processes' but does not mandate real OS-level subprocesses, so an implementer using threading produces a permanently false-green gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'the test MUST spawn two real OS-level subprocesses (e.g. via subprocess.Popen or multiprocessing.Process) — threading.Thread is insufficient because fcntl.flock is per-PID granular and threads within one process do not contend.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the Scope 'In' statement to explicitly include no_mutate behavioral tests (e.g. 'WS1-2 invariants plus no_mutate=True behavioral guard') and add a one-line note explaining why this WS3-surface test belongs in WS5's suite rather than WS3's.
findings.finding: The Scope 'In' declares coverage of 'WS1-2 invariants', but the no_mutate AC tests 'a library read-only fsck surface (no_mutate=True)' — which the Scope Out section associates with WS3 ('fsck byte-parity/help goldens (WS3)'). This creates a cross-section ambiguity about whether no_mutate is WS1-2 or WS3 scope.
findings.location: Scope section ('In: the behavioral test suite for WS1-2 invariants') vs no_mutate AC and Scope Out ('fsck byte-parity/help goldens (WS3)')
findings.suggested_fix: Change Scope In to: 'the behavioral test suite for WS1-2 invariants and the no_mutate=True sweep-guard (behavioral, not fsck byte-parity)' and add a parenthetical: '(no_mutate behavioral guard lives here because it tests sweep suppression, not fsck output correctness — that remains in WS3)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether AC3's two-process concurrency test should extend the existing `tests/integration/test_store_concurrency.py` harness (add an `ensures`-sweep case there) rather than introducing a new bespoke multi-process scaffold — cite the existing pattern or explain why a separate file is warranted.
findings.finding: The concurrency AC (AC3) specifies 'two real OS processes sweeping the same checkout serialize under the store write lock (_store/lock.py)' but does not acknowledge or extend the established cross-process test harness already in the codebase. The codebase has tests/integration/test_store_concurrency.py (multi-process write-lock storm via subprocess.Popen, integration mark, convergence assertion) and tests/unit/test_snapshot_cache.py::test_cross_process_single_flight (subprocess.Popen + shared marker file). Writing a bespoke two-process ensures-sweep scaffolding from scratch, without acknowledging these patterns, risks duplicating marker-file, env-scrubbing, and process-wait infrastructure that already exists — NIH of the test harness itself.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `test_concurrent_ensures_sweep_serializes` test case inside `tests/integration/test_store_concurrency.py`, reusing its existing `clone` fixture, `_clean_env()`, and subprocess.Popen pattern, and asserting `.ensure-applied` converges with no torn writes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate whether AC3's concurrency test extends `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py` or lives in a new file, and note the required `pytest.mark.integration` marker and any shared fixture dependencies.
findings.finding: The concurrency AC (AC3) specifies serialization under `_store/lock.py` but does not reference or align with the established cross-process concurrency test pattern in `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py`, which already exercises real OS subprocesses and the same write lock. This is an unenumerated external consumer context that the new tests must coexist with.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/interfaces/store/test_tracker_config_e2e.py` as an existing no_mutate caller and clarify whether AC4 extends that test or adds a separate assertion specifically covering the new `run_ensures` gate.
findings.finding: AC4 ('no_mutate test: a library read-only fsck surface mutates nothing and never runs the sweep') targets the `no_mutate=True` path of `fsck_cli`, but `tests/interfaces/store/test_tracker_config_e2e.py` already calls `fsck.fsck_cli([], repo_root=str(repo), no_mutate=True)`. The plan does not enumerate this existing caller as a potential overlap or consumer that may already partially cover AC4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default measurement mechanism for the '.ensure-applied' read-count assertion (e.g., 'patch builtins.open or the module's open call and assert call_count == 1').
findings.finding: The hot-path AC says '.ensure-applied' is 'read at most once per process' but does not state the defaultable mechanism by which the test measures this (e.g., patching builtins.open with a call counter, using unittest.mock, or checking a module-level cache hit metric), leaving the executor to choose an approach that may not match the design intent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Hot-path regression test bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'verified by patching the file-open call on `.ensure-applied` and asserting call_count <= 1 within a single process invocation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the defaultable OS-process mechanism for the concurrency test (e.g., 'use multiprocessing.Process or subprocess.Popen; pytest-xdist is not required') and note any platform constraints.
findings.finding: The concurrency AC specifies 'two real OS processes' but does not state the defaultable mechanism (multiprocessing.Process, subprocess.Popen, pytest-xdist workers, or a threading shim), nor does it state the platform constraint — leaving the executor to choose an approach that may be non-portable or incompatible with CI.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'implemented via multiprocessing.Process (or subprocess.Popen) on POSIX; document if Windows is unsupported for this test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the concurrency test detects torn/lost writes (e.g., 'after both processes complete, read .ensure-applied and assert its content is valid JSON / matches expected marker set with no partial lines').
findings.finding: The concurrency AC claims '.ensure-applied is written atomically (no torn/lost writes)' as a measurable outcome but provides no stated method to detect torn or lost writes in the test — making this criterion difficult to evaluate in-session with a deterministic assertion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'verified by reading .ensure-applied after both processes exit and asserting the file content is a well-formed marker set (e.g., valid JSON or expected line format) with no partial/empty content.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the direct coupling to `_store/lock.py` in the AC (or replace the path reference with a stable public/interface symbol), and note that if the lock module is renamed the concurrency test target must be updated in tandem.
findings.finding: The concurrency AC names `_store/lock.py` as the specific private internal module that must serialize processes, coupling the test specification — and therefore the tests themselves — directly to a private implementation path with no acknowledgment of this coupling or suggestion of an interface boundary to insulate tests from internal refactors.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Concurrency test AC: 'serialize under the store write lock (`_store/lock.py`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define who creates the shared test state (converged git repo, .ensure-applied, lock directory) for idempotency and concurrency tests — specify fixture ownership (e.g., a named pytest fixture using tmp_path) in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan does not define who creates the shared test state (converged git checkout, pre-populated .ensure-applied, lock directory) for the idempotency and concurrency tests — fixture ownership and CREATE semantics are absent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Idempotency test and Concurrency test bullets
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or implementation note: 'A shared pytest fixture `converged_store(tmp_path)` provisions a git repo, runs `run_ensures()` once to convergence, and writes `.ensure-applied`; the concurrency test reuses this fixture with two subprocess.Popen calls.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a RETIRE/cleanup path for all shared concurrency-test artifacts (checkout directory, .ensure-applied, lock files) — e.g., mandate use of `tmp_path`-scoped fixtures and explicit process.wait()/kill() in teardown.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no RETIRE/cleanup path for the shared artifacts (git checkout, .ensure-applied, lock files) created by the concurrency test's two real OS processes; leaked artifacts risk cross-test contamination in parallel CI runs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the concurrency AC: 'Test uses `tmp_path` for full filesystem isolation; a try/finally block ensures both subprocess handles are .wait()ed and the lock file is removed even on test failure.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit cross-reference in AC2 to the WS1 contract that specifies: (a) `run_ensures` must not be invoked by the ticket-mutation hot path, (b) no git-config subprocess is spawned by `run_ensures` itself, and (c) `.ensure-applied` is read at most once per process via a named cache — so the test has a grounded spec to assert against rather than asserting behaviors of non-existent code.
findings.finding: AC2 (hot-path regression test) makes three confident behavioral assertions — 'a common mutation never invokes `run_ensures`', 'spawns no git-config subprocess', and 'reads `.ensure-applied` at most once per process' — about `run_ensures` and `.ensure-applied`, neither of which exists anywhere in `src/rebar/`. Both are WS1 deliverables. Under the fail-closed rule, these are unverifiable codebase assertions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit target test-file path(s) for each AC (e.g., `tests/interfaces/store/test_ensures_behavioral.py`) so edit targets are verifiable and layout conflicts can be detected.
findings.finding: The plan names zero concrete test-file paths. No edit target (e.g., `tests/interfaces/store/test_ensures_idempotency.py` or any other path) is specified anywhere, making it impossible to verify that named files exist or to check for conflicts with the existing test layout.
findings.suggested_fix: Consolidate all five ACs into `tests/interfaces/store/test_ensures_behavioral.py` (one file per WS is the pattern already used) and name it explicitly in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale to the What section explaining why integration-style tests (real git checkouts, real OS processes) are preferred over mock-based unit tests for these ACs — e.g., 'real git operations are used to avoid over-coupling tests to internal APIs and to catch OS-level lock and atomicity behaviors that mocks cannot exercise.'
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for choosing integration-style behavioral tests (real git repos, real OS temp checkouts) over unit-test-with-mocks for the idempotency and concurrency ACs. The absence of a stated rationale is a G6.4 gap: a plan reviewer or future implementer cannot tell whether the integration approach was a deliberate design choice (e.g., to avoid over-specifying the internal API) or an oversight.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
